---
title: "Pipeline architecture"
description: "How a dataset flows from raw sequencing to an annotated object."
order: 60
---

OpenPipeline provides modular, best-practice Nextflow workflows for single-cell
data — from raw sequencing output to a batch-corrected, clustered, annotated
dataset. A dataset flows through the same shape whichever
[package](ecosystem.qmd) you use: ingestion, an optional QC report, per- and
multi-sample processing, integration, and downstream analysis, with a spatial
branch running in parallel.

```{mermaid}
%%| label: fig-architecture
%%| fig-cap: "A dataset flows from raw experimental data (grey) through ingestion, processing, integration, and downstream analysis. It can enter as raw FASTQ (after demultiplexing) or as a pre-built count matrix. A QC report is generated off the ingested data; the spatial branch runs the same shape with spatial-aware steps."
%%{init: {"theme":"base", "themeVariables":{"fontSize":"14px"}}}%%
flowchart TD
  classDef external fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#aaaaaa,color:#444
  classDef map     fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
  classDef ingest  fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
  classDef process fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
  classDef integ   fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d6b656,color:#000
  classDef down    fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
  classDef spatial fill:#d0f0f5,stroke:#3a9db5,color:#000
  classDef qc      fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#c9a227,color:#000

  wetlab["<b>Experiment &amp; sequencing</b><br>10x Chromium<br>BD Rhapsody<br>Xenium / CosMx (spatial)"]:::external
  fastq["<b>FASTQ files</b><br>or pre-built count matrices"]:::external

  map["<b>Demultiplexing &amp; mapping</b><br>demux (BCL to FASTQ)<br>cellranger_multi<br>bd_rhapsody<br>star_align"]:::map
  ingest["<b>Ingestion &amp; conversion</b><br>from_cellranger_multi_to_h5mu<br>conversion<br>to MuData (H5MU) per sample"]:::ingest
  qcreport["<b>QC reporting</b><br>generate_qc_report<br>(openpipeline_qc)"]:::qc
  process["<b>Process samples</b><br>filter_with_counts<br>cellbender_remove_background<br>clr / normalize / HVF<br>pca"]:::process
  integ["<b>Integration &amp; clustering</b><br>harmony_leiden<br>scvi_leiden<br>totalvi_leiden<br>bbknn_leiden<br>to Leiden clusters + UMAP"]:::integ
  down["<b>Downstream analysis</b><br>scanvi_scarches<br>celltypist<br>singler<br>lianapy<br>pseudobulk_deseq2"]:::down
  spatial["<b>Spatial branch</b><br>spaceranger_mapping<br>from_xenium_to_h5mu<br>spatial_process_samples<br>nichecompass"]:::spatial

  wetlab --> map --> ingest --> process --> integ --> down
  fastq -.-> ingest
  ingest -.-> qcreport
  ingest -.-> spatial
  spatial -.-> integ
```

## Demultiplexing

If your sequencing provider delivers raw Illumina BCL files, convert them to
per-sample FASTQ files at the start. This is **often already performed upstream**
by your provider, but is also available via the `ingestion/demux` workflow, which
supports **bcl2fastq**, **BCL Convert**, and **cellranger mkfastq** (selected with
`--demultiplexer`). If you already have FASTQ files or a pre-built count matrix,
skip straight to ingestion.

## Ingestion

Every cell is tagged with a unique barcode before sequencing. Ingestion aligns
the resulting short reads back to a reference genome, uses the barcode to assign
each read to a cell, and uses the UMI (Unique Molecular Identifier) to count each
RNA molecule exactly once — removing the amplification bias from PCR. The result
is a **count matrix** (cells × genes), stored — along with cell and gene metadata
and any additional modalities (ADT, VDJ, ATAC) — in a single **H5MU file per
sample**. This per-sample H5MU is the unit that flows through every later step.
Pre-built count matrices from Cell Ranger, Scanpy, or Seurat enter here through
the [converters](mudata.qmd#working-with-other-formats). See
[Ingest 10x Genomics data](../guides/ingest-10x.qmd) for workflow selection and
reference-genome setup.

## QC reporting

Directly off the ingested data, the [`openpipeline_qc`](ecosystem.qmd) package's
`generate_qc_report` produces a standalone HTML report of count distributions,
mitochondrial fractions, and doublet signals. Running it **before** processing
lets you inspect the raw data and choose filter thresholds deliberately rather
than accepting defaults. It is optional and sits to the side of the main flow.
See [Generate a QC report](../guides/qc-report.qmd).

## Process samples

Raw count matrices contain not only real cells but also empty droplets (ambient
RNA), damaged cells (high mitochondrial fraction), and doublets (two cells
sequenced as one). The `process_samples` workflow handles all per-sample and
multi-sample processing in a single command.

Per sample, each modality is split out and cells are flagged for removal on total
counts, number of detected genes, and mitochondrial fraction. For RNA, **doublet
detection** then runs with Scrublet, adding scores to `.obs` rather than removing
cells outright, so you keep control of the threshold. ADT (CITE-seq) data gets
the same count-based filtering without doublet detection.

After per-sample QC, samples are **concatenated together** — not normalized per
sample — because normalization needs to see variation across the whole cohort. A
`sample_id` column records each cell's origin. The concatenated object is then
normalized (scaled to a fixed total count, then log1p-transformed), reduced to
the 2,000–3,000 most **highly variable features**, and compressed with **PCA**
into the reduced space all integration methods operate in. For large cohorts,
`process_batches` runs the multi-sample steps on groups in parallel before
merging, reducing peak memory. See
[Process & filter samples](../guides/process-filter.qmd).

:::{.callout-note}
ATAC data is carried through `process_samples` without dedicated QC or
normalization — ATAC-specific processing is not yet implemented. ATAC ingestion
(`mapping/cellranger_atac_count`) and a standalone QC-metrics module
(`qc/calculate_atac_qc_metrics`) are available.
:::

## Integration

When samples come from different donors, runs, or library preparations, cells of
the same biological type cluster by their technical origin rather than their
biology. **Batch correction** realigns them in the PCA embedding so biology — not
technical source — drives the groupings. From the corrected embedding a
**k-nearest-neighbor graph** is built, **Leiden** community detection assigns
cluster labels, and **UMAP** projects the graph to 2D for visualization. Those
three outputs — corrected embedding, cluster labels, UMAP — are what downstream
tools consume. OpenPipeline provides one workflow per method; there is no single
default. See [Integrate with Harmony or scVI](../guides/integrate.qmd).

## Downstream and annotation

After integration the numbered Leiden clusters have no biological labels yet.
**Annotation** assigns cell-type identities — by comparing marker-gene expression
to known references, or by transferring labels from an annotated atlas. This runs
separately from the core pipeline and depends on the tissue and question. Beyond
annotation, the integrated H5MU is the starting point for differential
expression, RNA velocity, cell-cell communication, and population-level analyses
— some as OpenPipeline workflows, others as external tools that read the H5MU
directly. See [Annotate cell types](../guides/annotate.qmd).

## The spatial branch

Spatial data ([`openpipeline_spatial`](ecosystem.qmd)) follows the same shape
with spatial-aware steps. It ingests via `spaceranger_mapping` (Visium) or the
Xenium/CosMx converters, keeping per-spot or per-cell coordinates in
`.obsm["spatial"]`; runs spatial QC and `spatial_process_samples`; and can
identify recurring neighbourhoods with **NicheCompass**. From there it rejoins
the shared integration and downstream steps. See
[Ingest Xenium / Visium](../guides/ingest-spatial.qmd) and
[Find spatial niches](../guides/spatial-niches.qmd).

---

Browse every workflow and component, with full argument lists, in the
[reference](../reference/index.qmd).
